title: Lesson 7 - Capstone: Build, Validate, and Ship
description: Apply everything you've learned to deliver a production-grade skill. Validate it with tooling, run a cold test, and publish it for your team or the community.
learningObjectives: Build a complete skill with instructions, references, and scripts that aligns to your Lesson 1 charter.
Validate structure using the skills-ref CLI and behavioral quality using smoke tests.
Conduct a cold test where another person or AI tool uses the skill without coaching.
Ship the skill via git and establish a maintenance plan.

videos.title: Choosing and Scoping Your Capstone
videos.description: Select a compelling workflow, scope it realistically, and set clear success criteria for the session.
scriptOutline: Revisit your Lesson 1 charter — is this still the right workflow to automate?
If not, pick from four exemplar projects (or bring your own).
Review the charter template: problem, users, success metric.
Scope ruthlessly: solve ONE workflow extremely well. Out-of-scope is fine.
Connect to the scorecard: your capstone should target a workflow scoring 12+.
Set expectations: you'll iterate 2-3 times before sharing. Perfection is not the goal.
talkingPoints: Real value beats cleverness — pick a workflow you'll actually use Monday morning.
Scope ruthlessly. A narrow, excellent skill beats a broad, mediocre one.
Use your scorecard to defend your choice. If frequency + variance + risk is high, it's worth automating.

videos.title: The End-to-End Build Cycle
videos.description: Walk through the complete build process with checkpoints, demonstrating the finished deploy-checklist as reference.
scriptOutline: The 8-step build loop: define → structure → write → add references → test → iterate → validate → ship.
Show the completed deploy-checklist skill as the finished reference (all files, all sections).
Live demo: test with real inputs, capture deviations, fix instructions, re-test.
Run skills-ref validate to catch structural issues.
Prepare the PR: screenshots, test log, compatibility notes.
The cold test: hand the skill to someone who's never seen it. Can they use it?
talkingPoints: Validation tooling catches structure issues. Testing catches behavioral ones. You need both.
Document everything — reviewers shouldn't have to guess how to test your skill.
Ship even if it's not perfect. Iterate from real feedback, not hypothetical improvements.

videos.title: Maintenance, Composition, and What's Next
videos.description: Position the capstone as the beginning — cover maintenance, composing skills, and growing a skill library.
scriptOutline: Skills need maintenance: update when conventions change, teams grow, or tools evolve.
Version control helps: git history shows why a skill changed, PRs let the team review updates.
Composing skills: multiple skills work together — code-review → changelog → release-notes.
Building a skill library: how teams catalog, discover, and onboard new members to their skills.
Course summary: from repeated prompts to a portable, tested, production-grade skill.
What's next: the open standard is evolving, new tools keep adopting it, the community is growing.
talkingPoints: A skill is never 'done' — it evolves with your needs. That's why version control matters.
When you have 5-10 skills, they start composing: one skill's output feeds another's input.
You now have a superpower: the ability to teach your AI assistant anything, once, and have it remember forever.
Share your skills publicly when possible — community feedback is invaluable.

textSections.title: Capstone Project Options
textSections.content:
Choose one of these projects or bring your own:

### Option 1: Code Review Skill
**Problem:** Reviews are inconsistent — different reviewers check different things.
**What to build:** Automated review against your team's specific conventions.
**Include:** `references/style-guide.md` with conventions, output format as a checklist.
**Good for:** Developers, tech leads, anyone who reviews PRs.

### Option 2: Documentation Skill
**Problem:** Docs don't match your project's style or structure.
**What to build:** Generate docs following your project's format and voice.
**Include:** `references/doc-template.md`, `references/example-doc.md`.
**Good for:** Technical writers, product managers, anyone who writes docs.

### Option 3: Deploy Checklist Skill
**Problem:** Deployments miss steps — someone forgets migrations or env var checks.
**What to build:** Pre-deploy validation that catches issues before shipping.
**Include:** `scripts/pre-deploy-check.sh`, `assets/required-env-vars.json`.
**Good for:** DevOps, backend engineers, anyone who ships code.

### Option 4: Content Skill
**Problem:** Content doesn't match your brand voice or format.
**What to build:** Content creation following your brand's tone and structure.
**Include:** `references/brand-voice.md`, `references/content-template.md`.
**Good for:** Marketers, content creators, product managers.

### Option 5: Your Own
**Problem:** The workflow from your Lesson 1 charter.
**What to build:** Whatever saves you the most time.
**Include:** Whatever supporting files make sense.

textSections.title: Build Checklist
textSections.content:
Track your progress through the 8-step build cycle:

**Define (Lesson 1)**
- [ ] Problem statement: What pain does this skill solve?
- [ ] Target user: Who will use this skill?
- [ ] Success metric: How will you know it's working?

**Structure (Lesson 2)**
- [ ] Folder created with correct kebab-case naming
- [ ] SKILL.md has valid frontmatter (name matches folder, description is scenario-specific)

**Write (Lesson 3)**
- [ ] Trigger section with specific activation conditions
- [ ] Workflow with 4+ numbered steps, each starting with a verb
- [ ] Constraints with at least 2 things NOT to do
- [ ] Output section with concrete example

**Enhance (Lessons 4-6)**
- [ ] references/ added if needed (templates, examples)
- [ ] scripts/ added if needed (validation, automation)
- [ ] assets/ added if needed (data, configs)
- [ ] Platform features added if applicable ($ARGUMENTS, paths)
- [ ] Supporting Files table documented in SKILL.md

**Test & Ship (Lesson 7)**
- [ ] Tested 3+ times with different inputs
- [ ] Deviations logged and instructions fixed
- [ ] skills-ref validate passes (or manual validation checklist completed)
- [ ] Cold test: someone else used it without coaching
- [ ] Committed to git and shared (PR, repo, or community)

textSections.title: Capstone Quality Rubric
textSections.content:
Score your capstone on each dimension (aim for 4-5 on all):

| Dimension | 1-2 (Needs Work) | 3 (Acceptable) | 4-5 (Excellent) |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Problem Clarity** | Vague or too broad | Partially scoped | Crisp trigger + success metric |
| **Instruction Quality** | Missing sections | Basic structure, some gaps | All 4 parts with examples + quality gates |
| **Supporting Files** | None when needed | One directory used | References + scripts/assets, all documented |
| **Testing** | Untested | One manual pass | 3+ logged tests, deviations fixed |
| **Sharing** | Local only | In repo, no docs | PR + README + compatibility notes |

Use this rubric for self-assessment and peer review.

textSections.title: Validation Pipeline
textSections.content:
Run these checks before sharing:

**Automated:**
```bash
# Validate structure (if skills-ref is installed)
skills-ref validate ./my-skill

# Alternative: manual validation
# 1. Folder name matches 'name' field? ✓
# 2. Frontmatter valid YAML between --- markers? ✓
# 3. Name is lowercase with hyphens? ✓
# 4. Description under 1024 chars and scenario-specific? ✓
# 5. All file references use relative paths? ✓
# 6. Scripts are executable (chmod +x)? ✓
```

**Manual:**
- [ ] Skill tested 3+ times with different inputs
- [ ] Cold test completed (another person or AI tool used it successfully)
- [ ] Supporting Files table is complete and accurate
- [ ] No hardcoded paths, secrets, or machine-specific references

textSections.title: Course Summary: Your Journey
textSections.content:
Here's what you've learned across 7 lessons:

| Lesson | Key Takeaway | What You Built |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| 1. Why Skills Matter | Skills = reusable expertise, open standard across 30+ tools | Scorecard + charter |
| 2. Designing a Skill | SKILL.md + optional directories, progressive disclosure | Folder skeleton + frontmatter |
| 3. Crafting Instructions | Trigger → Workflow → Constraints → Output framework | Complete instruction body |
| 4. Deploying & Sharing | Personal → project → enterprise, shared via git | Promoted skill to project repo |
| 5. Power Features | references/, scripts/, assets/ for richer skills | Template + validation script |
| 6. Platform Enhancements | Standard first, platform features second | Cross-platform compatibility |
| 7. Build & Ship | Full cycle: define, build, test, iterate, validate, share | Production-grade capstone skill |

**You now have the ability to:**
- Capture your expertise as reusable, testable skills
- Share skills with your team via git
- Write skills that work across 30+ AI tools
- Build, test, iterate, and ship skills independently

Welcome to the skill-building community. The skill you built today is just the beginning.

commonMistakes.mistake: Skipping the charter and jumping straight to building
commonMistakes.explanation: Without a clear problem statement and success metric, you'll build something that 'works' but doesn't solve a real pain. Five minutes on the charter saves hours of unfocused work.

commonMistakes.mistake: Testing with toy inputs instead of real scenarios
commonMistakes.explanation: A skill that works with 'Hello World' may fail with real data. Use actual tasks from your workflow — that's where the edge cases live.
One test doesn't catch inconsistencies. Three tests with different inputs reveals whether the skill is truly reliable.
If the skill only works because YOU know the missing context, it's not ready to share. Have someone else use it without any coaching.
Ship version 1 when it works, not when it's perfect. Real feedback from real usage is more valuable than hypothetical improvements.
Skills drift as your workflow evolves. Set a reminder to review your skill quarterly. Update it or retire it — stale skills erode trust.

reflectionQuestions: Compare your Lesson 1 scorecard to your capstone result. How much time does the skill save per week?
What was the most surprising thing you learned about writing instructions for AI?
During the cold test, what did the tester struggle with? What does that tell you about your instructions?
What's the next skill you'll build after this capstone?
If your entire team adopted skill-based workflows, what would change about how you work together?

exercises.title: Build Your Capstone Skill
exercises.description: Use the build checklist to create a complete skill from scratch (or finish the one you've been building). Follow the 8-step cycle.
exercises.output: A complete skill directory with SKILL.md, supporting files, and validated structure.
successCriteria: SKILL.md has valid frontmatter and all four instruction sections populated.
At least one supporting file (reference, script, or asset) included and documented.
skills-ref validate passes (or manual validation checklist completed).
Supporting Files table present in SKILL.md.

exercises.title: The Cold Test
exercises.description: Hand your skill to another person (or a different AI tool) with NO explanation. Can they use it successfully from SKILL.md alone?
exercises.output: Feedback from the tester: what worked, what was confusing, and suggestions.
successCriteria: Tester completed the core workflow without verbal coaching from you.
Collected specific feedback on at least one confusing or missing element.
Applied at least one instruction improvement based on feedback.
Re-tested after the improvement to confirm the fix works.

exercises.title: Ship It
exercises.description: Commit your skill to a git repository, open a PR or publish it, and write a short announcement describing the skill.
exercises.output: Your skill live in a repository, accessible to at least one other person.
successCriteria: Skill committed to git with a descriptive commit message.
PR description (or README) includes: problem solved, how to invoke, test evidence.
Shared with at least one other person (teammate, community, or public repo).

instructorNotes: This is the payoff lesson. Allocate 60-70% of time to hands-on building. Keep lectures short.
The Cold Test (Exercise 2) is the most valuable exercise in the entire course. If time is limited, prioritize it over everything else.
Help stuck learners with: 'What did you copy-paste into your AI tool this week?' — that's their capstone.
For live sessions: end with a 2-3 minute skill showcase where each learner presents their capstone to the group.
Facilitate the cold test by pairing learners. Each person tests the other's skill — they learn from both giving and receiving feedback.
End with genuine encouragement: they've gone from zero skills to a working, tested, shareable skill in 7 lessons. The skill they built today is the first of many.
If learners want to continue: point them to community repositories for inspiration and suggest they build a skill library for their team.
